walkthrough.title: Sort the Card Decks
walkthrough.text: Let's get Meadow set up. First, find all the meadow cards. These are the beautifully illustrated nature cards depicting plants, insects, animals, and landscapes. Sort them by their card backs into separate decks. You will have multiple decks that feed into different rows and columns of the card grid.

walkthrough.title: Build the Card Grid
walkthrough.text: This is a four-by-four display of face-up cards arranged in four columns and four rows. Deal cards from the appropriate decks into each position so the grid is completely filled. This grid is the shared market where everyone will be claiming cards throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Understand Card Types
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to look at the cards in the grid. You will see three types distinguished by their border color. Ground cards have a brown border and form the foundation of your meadow. Green cards have a green border and build on top of ground cards. Sky cards have a blue border and sit atop green cards.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Observation Board
walkthrough.text: Find the observation board and place it near the card grid. Deal observation cards face up onto the board. These are special bonus cards that reward you for collecting specific sets of nature icons in your meadow. They provide significant victory points, so keep an eye on them.

walkthrough.title: Place the Campfire Board
walkthrough.text: Now set out the campfire board with its available bonus goals visible. The campfire board shows set collection targets that any player can race to complete. Place the campfire tokens nearby. These are first-come, first-served rewards, so popular goals may get snatched up quickly.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Path Tokens
walkthrough.text: Each player receives a complete set of numbered path tokens, numbered one through six. These tokens are how you will claim cards from the grid on your turn. Keep your path tokens in front of you where you can see them all.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Your Meadow Area
walkthrough.text: Each player needs a clear tableau area in front of them. This is your personal meadow where you will play cards throughout the game. You start with an empty meadow and no cards in hand. Everything you need will come from the shared grid.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick a random player to go first. Play will proceed clockwise. Make sure the remaining card decks are nearby for refilling the grid when cards are taken. Once the grid is full, the observation board is set, and everyone has their path tokens, you are ready to play.

summary.title: Sort the Card Decks
bullets: Sort meadow cards by card back into separate decks
These decks feed into different positions on the card grid

Sky cards (blue border) — top-tier, built on green

Each requires specific icon sets from your meadow

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Meadow is played over several rounds. On each turn, you will place one path token to claim one card from the shared grid, then either play it to your meadow or hold it in your hand. The game ends when all players have used all their path tokens for the final time. Let's walk through exactly how a turn works.

walkthrough.title: Place a Path Token
walkthrough.text: On your turn, choose one numbered path token from your supply and place it on a row or column marker around the card grid. This token determines which line of cards you have access to. If another player's token is already on that specific marker spot, you cannot place there. Choose a different row or column instead.

walkthrough.title: Claim a Card
walkthrough.text: After placing your path token, take one face-up card from the row or column you selected. You can pick any card along that line. This is the core action of every turn, so scan the grid carefully before committing your token.

walkthrough.title: Play or Hold the Card
walkthrough.text: Once you have claimed a card, you have two choices. You can play it immediately to your meadow if you meet its icon requirements. Or you can add it to your hand to play later. You may also play cards from your hand to your meadow on your turn if their requirements are met.

walkthrough.title: Card Requirements
walkthrough.text: Most cards require specific nature icons to be visible in your meadow before you can play them. Ground cards usually have no requirements or very simple ones, making them ideal starting points. Green cards require ground-level icons like mushrooms and flowers. Sky cards require higher-level icons like butterflies and feathers. Always check the requirement icons on a card before trying to play it.

walkthrough.title: The Layering System
walkthrough.text: Cards in your meadow follow a natural layering order. Ground cards go down first as your foundation. Green cards are played on top of or adjacent to ground cards. Sky cards are played on top of or adjacent to green cards. This creates a progression from ground to green to sky. When you play a card on top of another, it may cover icons you need for future plays, so plan your stacking carefully.

walkthrough.title: Refill the Grid
walkthrough.text: After you take a card from the grid, the remaining cards slide to fill the gap. Then a new card is drawn from the appropriate deck to refill the empty space. This means the grid is constantly changing, revealing new options every turn. Pay attention to what slides into place because it might be exactly what you need.

walkthrough.title: Claiming Observation Cards
walkthrough.text: As an additional option on your turn, you can claim an observation card from the observation board. To do this, your meadow must display all the specific icons required by that observation card. Observation cards are worth significant bonus victory points, so building toward them is a key part of your strategy.

walkthrough.title: Campfire Bonuses
walkthrough.text: The campfire board shows set collection goals that any player can claim. When your meadow displays the required icon set for a campfire goal, you can claim that campfire token. These tokens are first-come, first-served, so if you see a goal that matches your icons, grab it before someone else does. Each campfire token is worth bonus victory points.

walkthrough.title: Hand Management
walkthrough.text: Cards you claim but do not play immediately go into your hand. Be mindful of any hand limit that applies to your game edition. Holding too many cards without playing them can be wasteful since cards in hand score zero points. Try to play cards from your hand as soon as their requirements are met.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Each round, players take turns placing path tokens one at a time going clockwise. When all players have placed all their path tokens, the round ends. Path tokens are returned for the next round. New cards may be dealt to refresh the grid, and play continues.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the final round when all path tokens have been used for the last time. Add up your score from three categories. First, sum the victory points printed on all cards in your meadow. Second, add victory points from any observation cards you claimed. Third, add victory points from any campfire tokens you earned. The player with the most total victory points wins. If there is a tie, the player with more cards in their meadow wins.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: Multiple rounds — place path tokens to claim cards from shared grid
Play cards to your meadow or hold in hand
Game ends when all path tokens used in the final round

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Meadow
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through several turns with three example players so you can see how the game flows. Even if you have a different player count, follow along to understand the key decisions. Let's explore the meadow together.

walkthrough.title: Surveying the Grid
walkthrough.text: Before anyone places a path token, take a moment to look at the four-by-four card grid. Notice how some cards are ground cards with brown borders and simple or no requirements. Others are green or sky cards that need specific icons. On your first few turns, you want to grab ground cards because they form the foundation everything else builds on.

walkthrough.title: Player One Claims a Ground Card
walkthrough.text: Player one places a path token on a row that contains a ground card with a mushroom icon. They take the card and play it directly to their meadow. Since ground cards have minimal requirements, it goes down immediately. Now Player one has a mushroom icon showing in their meadow, which opens up green cards that need mushrooms.

walkthrough.title: Why Ground Cards Come First
walkthrough.text: This is the most important lesson in Meadow. Without ground cards in your meadow, you will never have the icons needed to play green or sky cards. Think of ground cards as planting seeds. They are rarely flashy, but everything else grows from them.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Goes for an Icon-Rich Card
walkthrough.text: This is a smart pick. Cards with multiple icons give you more flexibility for meeting future card requirements. One card with two icons is better than needing two separate cards for the same icons.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Chasing a Sky Card
walkthrough.text: Player three sees a beautiful sky card worth lots of victory points. They grab it even though they have zero ground cards and zero green cards in their meadow. The sky card requires butterfly and feather icons that only come from green cards. Player three is stuck holding a card they cannot play for several turns.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build from the Bottom Up
walkthrough.text: Never chase sky cards before you have the ground and green foundation to support them. That sky card will sit dead in Player three's hand while the other players are scoring points with cards they can actually play. Always build ground first, then green, then sky.

walkthrough.title: Player One Builds Upward
walkthrough.text: On their second turn, Player one scans the grid for a green card that requires a mushroom icon. They find one and claim it. Since they already have a mushroom icon showing from their ground card, they can play this green card immediately. Player one now has two cards in their meadow and a growing collection of icons.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's Mistake: Covering a Key Icon
walkthrough.text: Player two plays a green card on top of their ground card, covering the flower icon. They did not notice that a great sky card in the grid requires a flower icon. Now that flower is hidden under the green card and no longer counts. Player two just blocked their own future play.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch What You Cover
walkthrough.text: When you play a card on top of another, the covered icons disappear from your meadow. Before stacking, always check what icons the covered card provides and whether you still need them. Covering a key icon can cripple your entire strategy. Plan your stacking order carefully.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Recovers
walkthrough.text: Player three realizes their mistake and spends the next two turns claiming ground cards. They grab a card with a butterfly-friendly icon chain. It is not glamorous, but now they have a foundation. The sky card in their hand is still waiting, but at least there is a path toward playing it.

walkthrough.title: The Grid Shifts
walkthrough.text: Notice how the grid has changed after several turns. Cards slide to fill gaps and new cards appear from the decks. A card that was not available two turns ago might now be in a perfect row for your next path token. Always re-evaluate the grid before placing your token because the best option might have just appeared.

walkthrough.title: Player One Claims an Observation Card
walkthrough.text: Player one checks the observation board and realizes they have exactly the right icon combination showing in their meadow. They claim an observation card worth bonus victory points. This is a big moment. Observation cards provide significant points that can swing the game, and Player one earned it by building their icons methodically.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Races for a Campfire Token
walkthrough.text: Player two spots that they are one icon away from claiming a campfire bonus. They prioritize grabbing a card that completes the set, then claim the campfire token before anyone else. Campfire tokens are first-come, first-served. If Player two had waited one more turn, Player three might have claimed it instead.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Ignoring Observations
walkthrough.text: Player three has been so focused on catching up with their card chain that they have not looked at the observation board once. They have the icons to claim an observation card but did not notice. Always scan the observation board after playing a card to your meadow. Free points are only free if you remember to claim them.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Check Observations Every Turn
walkthrough.text: After every card you play to your meadow, glance at the observation board. Your icon collection changes with every card, and you might suddenly qualify for an observation you did not qualify for last turn. Making this a habit ensures you never miss bonus victory points.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen how to claim cards from the grid using path tokens, build a layered meadow of ground, green, and sky cards, and earn bonus points from observations and campfire goals. Remember the three key lessons: build from the bottom up, watch what you cover, and check observations every turn.

walkthrough.title: Before You Explore
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to icons, card types, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the most common mistakes new Meadow players make. First, skipping ground cards to chase flashy sky cards you cannot play yet. Second, covering key icons by stacking without thinking ahead. Third, ignoring observation cards and leaving free points on the table. Fourth, hoarding cards in hand without playing them. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Build a Strong Ground Foundation
walkthrough.text: Your first three or four turns should focus almost entirely on ground cards. Look for ground cards with multiple icons, because each icon unlocks different green cards. A ground card showing a mushroom and a flower opens up more future plays than a ground card with just one icon. Do not worry about victory point values on ground cards. Their real value is the icon chain they start.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Stacking Order
walkthrough.text: Before you play a card on top of another, ask yourself three questions. Do I still need the icons on the card being covered? Will I need those icons for an observation card or campfire goal? Is there a better position where I can play this card without covering something important? Taking ten seconds to answer these questions can save you from crippling your own strategy for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Path Token Timing
walkthrough.text: Your path tokens are numbered, and the numbers may affect priority or placement rules. Do not waste your best tokens early when the grid has mediocre options. Save your high-value tokens for turns when the grid has exactly what you need. Conversely, if a crucial card is available and you worry an opponent will take it, that is the time to spend a strong token.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Observation Board
walkthrough.text: Make it a habit to scan the observation board every single turn. After you play a card, your icon collection changes, and you might suddenly qualify for an observation you could not claim before. Observation cards provide the biggest bonus points in the game. Missing one because you forgot to look is the most avoidable mistake in Meadow.

walkthrough.title: Race for Campfire Tokens
walkthrough.text: Campfire bonuses are first-come, first-served. If you see a campfire goal that aligns with the icons you are naturally building, prioritize completing it. Do not assume you can grab it next round. Another player might be one card away from the same goal. When campfire races get tight, the player who adjusts their card picks to finish the set first wins those bonus points.

walkthrough.title: Balance VP and Icon Utility
walkthrough.text: Not every card needs to have high victory points. A two-point ground card with three useful icons might be more valuable than a five-point card with one icon, because those icons unlock three future plays. Think about cards as investments. In the early game, invest in icons. In the late game, cash in with high-VP cards that your icon collection can support.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Scoring
walkthrough.text: The strongest players in Meadow score from all three categories: meadow card victory points, observation cards, and campfire bonuses. Do not tunnel on just one. A player with good meadow cards plus two observation cards plus two campfire tokens will almost always beat a player who focused only on card VP. Spread your attention across all scoring opportunities.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't skip ground cards to chase sky cards you can't play
Don't cover key icons without checking future needs
Don't ignore observation cards — they're free bonus VP
Don't hoard cards in hand — unplayed cards = 0 points

summary.title: Balance VP and Icon Utility

Late game: cash in with high-VP cards your icons support
A 2 VP card with 3 icons can be worth more than a 5 VP card with 1

summary.title: Diversify Your Scoring

Don't tunnel on just one scoring method
Balanced scoring beats one-dimensional strategies

walkthrough.title: Icon Chain Planning
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think two or three cards ahead when selecting from the grid. Instead of grabbing the best card available right now, look at what cards are in the grid and plan a chain. If you can see a ground card that provides a mushroom, a green card that requires a mushroom and provides a butterfly, and a sky card that requires a butterfly, you have a three-card chain. Prioritize claiming the first link so you can build the entire chain over the next few turns.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Drafting
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best move is not taking the card you need most, but blocking an opponent from taking theirs. If you notice another player is one icon away from claiming a valuable observation card, and you can grab a card that would help them, consider taking it even if it is not your first choice. This is especially effective when campfire races are tight or when an opponent is about to claim a high-value observation.

walkthrough.title: Observation Card Targeting
walkthrough.text: At the start of the game, scan all available observation cards and identify which one or two are most achievable given the icons appearing in the grid. Build your meadow toward those specific icon combinations. An observation card worth eight victory points can be the difference between first and second place. Working backward from the observation requirements to your card picks is a hallmark of experienced play.

walkthrough.title: Efficient Stacking Layouts
walkthrough.text: Think of your meadow as a spatial puzzle. Ground cards that you plan to cover should be played first, while ground cards whose icons you need long-term should be placed where they will stay exposed. Some players create a wide foundation of ground cards first, then selectively stack green and sky cards only on ground cards whose icons are no longer needed. This preserves maximum icon availability throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Path Token Sequencing
walkthrough.text: Advanced players pay close attention to which path token they use each turn. If a particular row or column is contested, using a higher-numbered token ensures you get priority access. If the grid state is not urgent, save your strong tokens for later turns when competition heats up. Reading your opponents' remaining tokens tells you which spots they can compete for and which are safe for your lower-value tokens.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game VP Maximization
walkthrough.text: In the final round, shift your focus entirely to raw victory points. Stop worrying about icon chains and instead claim the highest-VP cards you can still play. If you have unplayed cards in your hand that meet their requirements, get them into your meadow now. Every card sitting in your hand at game end is worth zero. Check one last time for any observation cards or campfire goals you can claim before the game ends.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Grid Flow
walkthrough.text: The sliding refill mechanic means that taking a card from one position causes a predictable chain of card movements. Advanced players learn to anticipate which cards will slide into which positions after a pick. Sometimes taking a card from a specific spot causes another card to slide into a row or column that benefits you next turn. Use the grid dynamics to your advantage rather than simply picking the best card in isolation.

walkthrough.title: Multi-Goal Optimization
walkthrough.text: The best Meadow players identify cards that serve multiple purposes simultaneously. A single card pick might add victory points to your meadow, provide an icon needed for an observation card, and complete a campfire set collection goal. When you find a card that hits two or three objectives at once, that is almost always the correct pick regardless of its individual VP value. Seek these multi-purpose plays every turn.

summary.title: Icon Chain Planning
bullets: Think 2-3 cards ahead when picking from the grid
Identify ground → green → sky chains in the current grid
Claim the first link to enable the entire chain

summary.title: Defensive Drafting
bullets: Block opponents from key cards when stakes are high
Especially effective during campfire races and observation claims
Sometimes denying a card matters more than gaining one

summary.title: Observation Card Targeting
bullets: Scan all observation cards at game start
Pick 1-2 achievable targets and build toward them
Work backward from observation requirements → card picks
8+ VP observation cards can decide the game

summary.title: Efficient Stacking Layouts
bullets: Play ground cards you plan to cover first
Keep long-term icon providers exposed and uncovered
Wide foundation → selective stacking preserves icon access

summary.title: Path Token Sequencing
bullets: Higher tokens = priority access to contested rows/columns
Save strong tokens for high-competition turns
Read opponents' remaining tokens to gauge threats

summary.title: Late-Game VP Maximization
bullets: Final round: raw VP over icon building
Play all hand cards that meet requirements — hand = 0 VP
Last check for unclaimed observations and campfire goals

summary.title: Reading the Grid Flow
bullets: Sliding refill creates predictable card movements
Taking from specific spots can slide desired cards into your reach
Use grid dynamics as strategy, not just happenstance

summary.title: Multi-Goal Optimization
bullets: Seek cards that serve 2-3 goals simultaneously
VP + observation icon + campfire set = ideal pick
Multi-purpose plays almost always beat single-purpose picks

entries.definition: A card with a brown border that forms the foundation layer of your meadow. Ground cards have no or minimal icon requirements, making them the first cards you should play. They provide icons needed for green cards.
A card with a green border representing the middle tier of your meadow. Green cards require ground-level icons such as mushrooms and flowers. They provide icons needed for sky cards.

A card with a blue border representing the top tier of your meadow. Sky cards require green-level icons such as butterflies and feathers. They are typically the highest-VP cards but require the most prerequisite icons.

A special card from the observation board that requires a specific set of icons visible in your meadow. Observation cards provide significant bonus victory points and are claimed as an additional action on your turn.

The four-by-four display of face-up meadow cards arranged in columns and rows. Players place path tokens on rows or columns to claim cards from the grid. Cards slide to fill gaps, and new cards are drawn to refill empty spaces.
A separate board displaying observation cards available to claim. When your meadow icons match an observation card's requirements, you can claim it for bonus VP as an additional action on your turn.
A board showing set collection goals with first-come, first-served bonus tokens. When your meadow displays the required icon set, claim a campfire token for bonus VP. Popular goals get taken quickly.
Numbered tokens (one through six) each player uses to claim cards from the grid. Place one token per turn on a row or column marker. When all tokens are used, the round ends.
A reward token earned by completing a set collection goal on the campfire board. Each token is worth bonus victory points. Campfire tokens are limited and first-come, first-served.
A common nature icon found on ground cards. Required by many green cards as a prerequisite for placement. One of the most important early-game icons to collect.
A nature icon found on ground and green cards. Required by various green and sky cards. Cards with flower icons open up multiple building paths.
A nature icon representing animal tracks. Found on various card types and required by cards depicting animals higher in the food chain.
A nature icon typically found on green cards. Required by many sky cards as a prerequisite. Building toward butterfly icons unlocks the highest-tier cards.
A nature icon associated with birds and sky-level cards. Required by several high-VP sky cards. Often appears on green cards that serve as stepping stones to sky cards.

The system of stacking cards in your meadow. Ground cards go first, green cards build on ground, and sky cards build on green. Playing a card on top of another covers the lower card's icons, removing them from your visible collection.

The specific nature icons that must be visible in your meadow before you can play a card. Requirements are printed on each card. You must have all required icons showing in your meadow to play the card.
When a card is taken from the grid, remaining cards slide to fill the gap, then a new card is drawn to fill the empty space. This creates a dynamic grid that changes every turn.
Victory points printed on each card in your meadow. One of the three final scoring categories. Sum the VP values on all cards you have played to your meadow.
Victory points from claimed observation cards. One of the three final scoring categories. Observation cards provide significant bonus points for collecting specific icon sets.
Victory points from claimed campfire tokens. One of the three final scoring categories. Earned by completing set collection goals on the campfire board before other players.
If two or more players tie on total victory points, the player with more cards in their meadow wins. If still tied, players share the victory.

Your personal play area where cards are placed throughout the game. Your meadow displays the icons you have collected, which determine what future cards you can play. Cards in your meadow score their printed VP at game end.

A sequence of cards where each card provides the icons needed by the next card in the chain. For example, a ground card providing a mushroom enables a green card requiring a mushroom, which provides a butterfly enabling a sky card requiring a butterfly.
Cards you have claimed from the grid but not yet played to your meadow. Cards in hand score zero points at game end. Play them as soon as their requirements are met.
